title: Check Bytes and Inodes Before Cleanup
hook: No space left can mean full bytes, full inodes, or both.
problem: A host is failing writes and you need to know whether byte usage or inode usage is the tighter limit before deleting anything.
starting_state: The fixture reports high byte usage and high inode usage on the same mounted path.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints filesystem byte and inode summaries.
when_to_use: Use at the start of disk incidents before deciding whether to hunt large files or huge counts of small files.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat df output as a cleanup plan; it only identifies the pressured filesystem.
undo: No undo needed because both checks are read-only.
expected_output: Two filesystem tables: one for byte usage and one for inode usage.

shorts.title: Check bytes and inodes.
shorts.voiceover: Before deleting anything, check both df views. A filesystem can have free space and still run out of inodes.
shorts.caption: Disk pressure has two counters.

linkedin.question: During disk incidents, do you check inode pressure before cleanup?
linkedin.cta: Start with byte and inode evidence.

youtube.description: Use df and df -i together before choosing a disk cleanup strategy.

experiments.variant_a: No space left has two meanings.
experiments.variant_b: Check bytes and inodes before cleanup.

title: Keep du on One Filesystem
hook: A cleanup scan should not wander into mounted backups or network storage.
problem: You need a directory-level size view without crossing filesystem boundaries during disk triage.
starting_state: The fixture has logs, temporary uploads, and cache data under one application tree.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command measures apparent usage and keeps the scan on one filesystem.
when_to_use: Use after df identifies the pressured mount and you need a scoped directory ranking.
when_not_to_use: Do not run broad recursive scans from / during a busy incident unless the extra I/O is acceptable.
undo: No undo needed because du only reads metadata and file sizes.
expected_output: A human-readable size list for direct children under the selected var directory.

shorts.title: Scope your disk scan.
shorts.voiceover: After df names the full mount, use du with xdev behavior and a max depth so the scan stays targeted.
shorts.caption: Keep cleanup scans scoped.

linkedin.question: Do your disk cleanup scans intentionally avoid mounted backup paths?
linkedin.cta: Use scoped du output before drilling deeper.

youtube.description: Use scoped du output to rank directories without crossing filesystem boundaries.

experiments.variant_a: Do not scan the whole machine.
experiments.variant_b: Keep du on the pressured filesystem.

title: Preview Old Temp Files Before Deleting
hook: The safe version of cleanup is a candidate list first.
problem: A temporary upload directory may contain stale files, but you need evidence before removing anything.
starting_state: The fixture contains one old export and one recent export in a temporary upload path.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints only candidate files older than the threshold.
when_to_use: Use before manual cleanup of upload, export, cache, or temp paths where age matters.
when_not_to_use: Do not pipe this directly to rm until owners, retention rules, and open-file state are understood.
undo: No undo needed because the command only prints candidates.
expected_output: Old candidate files with modification date, byte size, and path.

shorts.title: Print cleanup candidates first.
shorts.voiceover: Use find to print old temp files before deletion enters the conversation. Age and size make the review concrete.
shorts.caption: Candidate list first, delete later.

linkedin.question: What proof do you require before deleting temp files on a production host?
linkedin.cta: Print the candidate list before cleanup.

youtube.description: Use find with age, size, and path output to preview old temporary files before cleanup.

experiments.variant_b: Preview old temp files before rm.

title: Find Directories Burning Inodes
hook: Inode cleanup starts by finding the directory with too many files.
problem: Inode usage is high and you need to locate which directories contain the most regular files.
command: find /lab/disk-inode-cleanup/var/cache/app -xdev -type f -printf '%h\n' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr | head
starting_state: The fixture contains many small cache files split across shard directories.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command counts files by parent directory.
when_to_use: Use when df -ih is high and large-file searches do not explain write failures.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume the largest count is safe to delete; identify the application owner and cache semantics first.
undo: No undo needed because this is a read-only file count.
expected_output: A count of regular files grouped by parent directory, sorted highest first.

commands: find /lab/disk-inode-cleanup/var/cache/app -xdev -type f -printf '%h\n' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr | head

shorts.title: Find inode hotspots.
shorts.voiceover: When inodes are the problem, count files by directory. The biggest byte consumer may not be the inode culprit.
shorts.caption: Inode pressure is file count pressure.

linkedin.question: Have you seen a small cache directory exhaust inodes before it exhausted bytes?
linkedin.cta: Count files by directory before deleting.

youtube.description: Use find, sort, and uniq to locate directories with high file counts during inode pressure.

title: List Empty Directories as Cleanup Candidates
hook: Empty directories are low-risk candidates, but they still deserve a preview.
problem: A cache tree may contain abandoned empty directories and you need to review them before cleanup.
starting_state: The fixture has an intentionally empty cache directory beside active shard directories.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints empty directories without removing them.
when_to_use: Use before pruning empty cache or working directories after a migration, deploy, or failed job.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete empty directories that applications expect to exist unless startup or deploy code recreates them.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Paths to empty directories under the selected cache tree.

shorts.title: Preview empty dirs.
shorts.voiceover: Even empty-directory cleanup should start with a print-only pass. Some apps expect those paths to exist.
shorts.caption: Empty does not always mean disposable.

linkedin.question: Do you treat empty directories as safe to remove, or do you check app expectations first?

youtube.description: Use find to list empty directories before deciding whether cleanup is safe.

title: Exclude the Current Release from Cleanup
hook: Release cleanup should prove what current points to before listing old directories.
problem: Several release directories exist and you need to identify stale candidates without touching the active symlink target.
starting_state: The fixture has dated release directories and a current symlink pointing at the newest release.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command resolves the active release and prints only non-current candidates.
when_to_use: Use before release-directory cleanup on hosts that keep multiple deploy versions.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete old releases until rollback policy, backup state, and active symlinks are confirmed.
undo: No undo needed because the command only resolves and prints paths.
expected_output: Older release directories, excluding the current symlink target.

shorts.title: Protect current release.
shorts.voiceover: Before cleaning old releases, resolve the current symlink and exclude that target from the candidate list.
shorts.caption: Release cleanup starts with current.

linkedin.question: What is your guardrail before deleting old release directories?
linkedin.cta: Resolve current first, then list candidates.

youtube.description: Use readlink and find to list stale release directories while excluding the active release.

experiments.variant_b: Do not delete the active release.

title: Rank Old Cleanup Candidates by Size
hook: The oldest file is not always the file that buys back meaningful space.
problem: You have old files in a cleanup path and need to review the largest candidates first without deleting anything.
starting_state: The fixture contains old logs, an old upload export, recent files, and many small cache files.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints old files with byte sizes and sorts the candidates largest first.
when_to_use: Use when cleanup pressure is urgent and you need to focus review on candidates that would actually reclaim space.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete the largest old file just because it is large; confirm ownership, retention, and whether a process still needs it.
undo: No undo needed because this command only prints candidate files.
expected_output: A largest-first list of old files with byte counts, dates, and paths.

shorts.title: Rank old files by size.
shorts.voiceover: If cleanup needs space back quickly, sort old candidates by bytes first. Review the big wins before touching anything.
shorts.caption: Old and large deserves review first.

linkedin.question: When disk cleanup is urgent, do you review oldest files first or largest old files first?
linkedin.cta: Sort candidates by impact before deletion.

youtube.description: Use find and sort to rank old cleanup candidates by size without deleting files.

experiments.variant_a: Old is not the same as useful to delete.
experiments.variant_b: Rank old cleanup candidates by size.

title: Review Log Files Before Cleanup
hook: Before truncating logs, prove which log files are large and how old they are.
problem: Log files are suspected during disk pressure and you need a compact review list before changing retention or truncating anything.
starting_state: The fixture has nginx log files with different sizes under a var/log tree.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints log file sizes, dates, and paths for review.
when_to_use: Use before truncating, compressing, rotating, or moving logs during disk incidents.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat log cleanup as a substitute for fixing missing rotation or runaway logging.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A size-sorted list of log files with modification dates and paths.

shorts.title: Review logs before cleanup.
shorts.voiceover: Do not blindly truncate logs during a disk alert. Print size, age, and path first so the cleanup decision is visible.
shorts.caption: Measure logs before touching them.

linkedin.question: Do you check log size and age before truncating during disk incidents?

youtube.description: Use find to review log file sizes and ages before cleanup.

experiments.variant_b: Review log size and age first.

title: Summarize Cache File Ages
hook: Cache cleanup is safer when you know whether files are stale or still active.
problem: A cache tree is using many inodes and you need to see file age distribution before deciding on a retention threshold.
starting_state: The fixture has many small cache files distributed across shard directories.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command groups cache files by modification date.
when_to_use: Use before age-based cache cleanup so the cutoff is based on the actual file population.
when_not_to_use: Do not use file age alone for application-owned caches that have their own invalidation or lock semantics.
undo: No undo needed because this is a read-only summary.
expected_output: Counts of cache files grouped by date.

shorts.title: Check cache age first.
shorts.voiceover: Before choosing a cache cleanup cutoff, count files by date. It turns a risky guess into a visible threshold.
shorts.caption: Choose cleanup cutoffs from evidence.

linkedin.question: Do you choose cache cleanup thresholds from file age data or from habit?

youtube.description: Use find and uniq to summarize cache file ages before inode cleanup.

experiments.variant_b: Count cache files by age first.

title: Print a Dry-Run Removal Script
hook: The reviewable cleanup command is the one you print before you run.
problem: You have cleanup candidates and need a human-reviewable removal plan instead of executing deletion directly from find.
starting_state: The fixture has an old temporary upload candidate and a recent upload that should not be listed.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints interactive rm commands as text and does not execute them.
when_to_use: Use when turning a candidate list into a reviewed cleanup plan for a small, well-understood path.
when_not_to_use: Do not pipe generated removal commands into a shell; review, quote, and execute intentionally if cleanup is approved.
undo: No undo needed for the dry run. If a printed command is later executed, restore from backup or the source system.
expected_output: One printed rm -i command per old cleanup candidate.

shorts.title: Print cleanup commands first.
shorts.voiceover: If deletion is on the table, print the exact rm commands first. Review the plan before anything touches the files.
shorts.caption: Dry-run cleanup as text.

linkedin.question: Do you review generated cleanup commands before executing them?
linkedin.cta: Print the removal plan before running it.

youtube.description: Use find to print a dry-run removal plan instead of deleting files directly.

experiments.variant_b: Never pipe cleanup straight into a shell.
